title: FAD
description: Flavin Adenine Dinucleotide (FAD) is a critical redox-active coenzyme derived from riboflavin (vitamin B2) that functions as a prosthetic group for various flavoproteins. It plays a central role in the mitochondrial electron transport chain and the Krebs cycle, facilitating essential energy production and metabolic homeostasis through cellular oxidation-reduction reactions.

research.title: The role of NAD+ metabolism and its modulation of mitochondria in aging and disease
research.summary: This review explores how essential cofactors like NAD+ and related redox molecules support mitochondrial health and homeostatic components like mitophagy to combat age-related decline.

research.title: Emerging strategies, applications and challenges of targeting NAD+ in the clinic
research.summary: Researchers discuss the diverse roles of pyridine and flavin-related nucleotides in signaling pathways, epigenetic regulation, and energy homeostasis as clinical targets for aging.

research.title: Effect of Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide on Heart Failure Caused by Ischemic Cardiomyopathy: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial
research.summary: Sub-study analysis indicates that boosting redox cofactors improves cardiac bioenergetics and clinical outcomes like ejection fraction in patients with heart failure.

sentiment.summary: Users generally view FAD and its precursors as highly effective for sustaining mitochondrial energy, though many note it is often best used as part of a broader B-vitamin or NAD-boosting stack.

effects.label: Bioavailability:
effects.description: Evidence suggests that active forms like FAD may be more effective than simple riboflavin for individuals with specific genetic mutations (e.g., MTHFR).

effects.label: Syntropic Benefits:
effects.description: Most effective when paired with other mitochondrial supports like CoQ10 or NAD+ precursors.

effects.label: Onset:
effects.description: Users typically report feeling metabolic changes within 1-2 weeks of consistent supplementation.

effects.label: Clinical Support:
effects.description: Growing clinical interest in using redox cofactors to treat cardiac and metabolic dysfunctions.

dosage.label: Active Forms:
dosage.description: Supplements providing 50mg to 100mg of riboflavin-5-phosphate or FAD are commonly used to ensure cofactor availability.

dosage.label: Stacked Dose:
dosage.description: Lower doses (10-25mg) are frequent when integrated into a high-quality B-complex.

dosage.label: Precursor Loading:
dosage.description: Some users prefer high-dose riboflavin (up to 400mg) under medical supervision for specific conditions like migraines.

sideEffects.label: Chromaturia:
sideEffects.description: A harmless but bright yellow-orange discoloration of urine is the most frequently reported effect.

sideEffects.label: Digestive Discomfort:
sideEffects.description: Rare reports of mild nausea if taken on an empty stomach.

sideEffects.label: Overstimulation:
sideEffects.description: Occasional reports of difficulty sleeping if taken too late in the evening due to increased ATP production.

availability.label: Direct Sourcing:
availability.description: FAD is less common as a standalone supplement than riboflavin or R5P but is available from specialty chemical and longevity vendors.

availability.label: Regulatory Status:
availability.description: Generally recognized as safe (GRAS) when sold as a form of Vitamin B2 in the United States.
